revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:五畿内志]]
[[File:5 Provinces of Japan Kinai.svg|thumb|250px|The five provinces of {{ill|Kinai|en|Kinai|qid=Q906708|lt=Kinai|1=Kinai}}]] The '''Gokinaishi''' (五畿内志), formally titled '''Nihon Yochi Tsuushi Kinai-bu''' (日本輿地通志畿内部), is a {{ill|Geography|en|Geography|qid=Q1071|lt=geographical|1=Geography}} record of the five provinces of {{ill|Kinai|en|Kinai|qid=Q906708|lt=Kinai|1=Kinai}} compiled during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}'s [[:en:Kyōhō era|Kyōhō era]] (1716–1736). It comprises the texts known as the [[Kawachi-shi]], [[Izumi-shi]], [[Settsu-shi]], [[Yamashiro-shi]], and [[Yamato-shi]]. Regarded as the first geographically focused text sanctioned by the [[:en:Edo shogunate|Edo shogunate]], it influenced later geographical compilation projects significantly.
== Overview == The Gokinaishi, abbreviated from Nihon Yochi Tsuushi Kinai-bu, was planned by {{ill|Seki Sokō|ja|関祖衡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seki Sokō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Namikawa Seisho|ja|並河誠所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Namikawa Seisho|1=Namikawa Seisho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q6563872}}. After Seki's death, Namikawa led the compilation process. Although intended as a comprehensive geographic survey of Japan, the work was limited to the Kinai region, leading to its abbreviated title. According to the introduction dated Kyōhō 19 (1734), the project spanned five years starting in Kyōhō 14 (1729), and its publication took place between Kyōhō 20 and 21 (1735–1736) in Osaka, Kyoto, and Edo.<ref name="Fujimoto_1985">Fujimoto [1985]</ref>
Comprising 61 volumes written in {{ill|Classical Chinese|en|Classical Chinese|qid=Q37041|lt=Classical Chinese|1=Classical Chinese}}, the work emulates the format of China's {{ill|Da Ming Yitong Zhi|ja|大明一統志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Da Ming Yitong Zhi|1=明一統志|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q9914604}}.<ref name="Nishimura_1956">Nishimura [1956]</ref><ref name="Fujimoto_1985"/> Each provincial section begins with illustrated maps of mountains, key roads, rivers, and district names, followed by descriptions of administrative history, boundaries, roads, scenic spots, customs, anomalies, villages, natural resources, temples, ancient sites, tombs, and clan details.<ref name="Wada_1993">Wada [1993]</ref>
Namikawa and his collaborators conducted field surveys and compiled ancient documents, records, and oral traditions to create detailed and precise descriptions. Their methods ensured the text's widespread citation in subsequent Kinai geographies and {{ill|Meisho Zue|ja|名所図会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meisho Zue|1=meisho zue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q3304753}}. The Gokinaishi also influenced later geographical texts like the {{ill|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpen Musashi Fudoki|1=Shinpen Musashi Fudoki-kō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11503943}} and {{ill|Shinpen Sagami Fudoki|ja|新編相模国風土記稿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpen Sagami Fudoki|1=Shinpen Sagami no Kuni Fudoki-kō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11503942}}, while providing valuable insight into early modern Kinai.<ref name="Fujimoto_1985"/><ref name="Wada_1993"/>
== Compilation History == === Early Context === {{See also|Genroku Kuniezu}} From the [[:en:Genroku era|Genroku era]] (1688–1704) to the Kyōhō era, Japan's geographical studies emerged amidst shifts in East Asian geopolitics, particularly the [[:en:Ming-Qing transition|Ming-Qing transition]].<ref>Shirai [2004: 65]</ref> Following the [[:en:fall of the Ming dynasty|fall of the Ming dynasty]] and the {{ill|Qing dynasty|en|Qing dynasty|qid=Q8733|lt=Qing dynasty|1=Qing dynasty}}'s rise, Japan's intellectuals reconsidered their nation's geography within the broader regional changes.<ref name="Tsukamoto_1982_46">Tsukamoto [1982: 46]</ref>
The creation of the {{ill|Genroku Kuniezu|ja|元禄国絵図|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genroku Kuniezu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in 1696 exemplifies the shogunate's interest in territorial understanding. Unlike earlier maps emphasizing domains and feudal divisions, the Genroku maps standardized national boundaries, reflecting a shift toward a unified conception of Japan.<ref>Sugimoto [1999: 168–195]</ref> This period also saw Japan's inclusion of the {{ill|Ryukyu Kingdom|en|Ryukyu Kingdom|qid=Q28025|lt=Ryukyu Kingdom|1=Ryukyu Kingdom}} in its maps, symbolizing growing national consciousness.<ref>Kawamura [1990: 226]</ref>
=== 18th-Century Geographic Thought === Intellectuals like {{ill|Dazai Shundai|ja|太宰春台|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dazai Shundai|1=Dazai Shundai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q6563258}} highlighted geography's importance to governance in works such as ''Keiseiroku'' (1729). Shundai lamented the absence of comprehensive Japanese geographic surveys and advocated for their compilation to facilitate governance and adjudication.<ref>Shirai [2004: 67]</ref>
Similarly, scholars like {{ill|Tani Taisan|ja|谷泰山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tani Taisan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} discussed geography in relation to political and cultural shifts, debating the risks and benefits of detailed surveys. These discussions framed the intellectual environment for Namikawa's project.<ref>Shirai [2004: 67]</ref>
=== Tokugawa Yoshimune's Cultural Policies === The Gokinaishi benefited from the su11ort of {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshimune|en|Tokugawa Yoshimune|qid=Q319664|lt=Tokugawa Yoshimune|1=Tokugawa Yoshimune}}'s administration, which valued practical scholarship. Yoshimune's interest in geographic studies tied to broader initiatives like the national medicinal herb survey (薬草見分), which underscored geography's utility for governance.<ref>Oishi [2001: 68–75]</ref>
Namikawa's survey efforts, su11orted by the shogunate, exemplified these policies. His investigations prioritized shrines, burial sites, and toponyms, while also establishing commemorative monuments for historical figures.<ref>Shirai [2004: 86]</ref>
=== Compilation and Publication === Namikawa completed ''Kawachi-shi,'' ''Izumi-shi,'' and ''Settsu-shi'' by 1733, presenting them to the shogunate. The remaining sections, ''Yamashiro-shi'' and ''Yamato-shi,'' followed in 1734. Although independently produced, the Gokinaishi received significant shogunal su11ort and was later regarded as quasi-official.<ref>Shirai [2004: 97]</ref>
== Significance and Legacy == The Gokinaishi, modeled after the ''Da Ming Yitong Zhi,'' pioneered systematic geographic surveys in Japan. Its influence extended to later works, inspiring both official and private geographical compilations. The text's integration of history and geography marked a shift toward using geography as a tool for political and cultural identity.<ref>Shirai [2004: 95]</ref>
== Bibliography == === Editions === * First published: Kyōhō 20–21 (1735–1736) * Volumes: ''' 1–10: ''Yamashiro-shi'' ''' 11–26: ''Yamato-shi'' ''' 27–43: ''Kawachi-shi'' ''' 44–48: ''Izumi-shi'' ''' 49–61: ''Settsu-shi''
=== Modern Editions === * Namikawa, Naga, and Masamune, Atsuo (1929–1930). ''Gokinaishi'' (3 vols.). Nihon Koten Zenshū Kankōkai. * Ashida, Kazuo (1929). ''Dai Nihon Chishi Taikei'' (Vol. 18). Yūzankaku.
== See Also == * {{ill|Tsubaki Bunsho|ja|椿井文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsubaki Bunsho|1=Tsubai Documents|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q105793781}}
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== External Links == * [https://www.pref.kyoto.jp/dezi/data/26.html ''Yamashiro-shi'' (compiled by Seki Sokō)] – Kyoto Prefectural General Archives Digital Exhibition "Pioneers of Kyoto Research"
